text: This is a case of pediatric appendicectomy. She is a seven-year-old girl with acute appendicitis.
The surgeon creates a small stab wound over the inferior crease of the umbilicus and inserts a small-sized Veress needle.
The surgeon listens for two click sounds, then performs irrigation and suction, followed by the hanging drop test to confirm proper needle placement.
The surgeon performs irrigation suction and the hanging drop test, using fluid to confirm the placement of the needle in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon initiates pneumoperitoneum by sucking in air through the inserted needle at the umbilical crease, preparing the abdominal cavity for laparoscopic access.
The surgeon introduces a trocar into the abdomen of a pediatric patient, following insufflation with 600 to 700 ml of CO2.
The surgeon visualizes the appendix, which is positioned retrocecal and pelvic, with noticeable free fluid surrounding it. The tip of the appendix appears slightly inflamed.

The surgeon observes fluid around the appendix, which appears kinked and elongated. The appendix is held in the middle, and the tip is visible. The harmonic scalpel is introduced for further dissection.

The surgeon positions the vibrating jaw of the harmonic scalpel towards the iliac vessel, ensuring the silicon jaw faces the vessel for dissection. The ilium is visible below, and the tinea coli can be seen on the cecum.
The surgeon navigates towards the deep base of the appendix, which is not yet reached. The intra-abdominal bladder is visible, typical in pediatric patients.

The surgeon carefully inserts a trocar into the left iliac fossa, revealing the base of the appendix. The major appendix is dissected up to its base using only two ports and a Striker mini alligator instrument.

The surgeon uses a striker mini alligator in the right hypochondrium to provide traction, allowing visualization of the base of the appendix. The dissection reaches the complete origin of the appendix to prevent stump appendicitis.

The surgeon uses a Melgers knot to secure the base of the long, inflamed appendix. A striker mini alligator is introduced into the loop, and the appendix is carefully threaded through it. The knot pusher is positioned at the base to ensure the loop tightens properly, preventing any stump from being left.

The surgeon tightens the knot around the base of the appendix two or three times with moderate tension before cutting it with scissors. The harmonic device is then used to dissect and cut the appendix, ensuring the mucosa is cauterized to prevent mucosal exposure. The stump is left at a length of three to five millimeters, and the appendix is visibly separated.

The surgeon changes the telescope to a 5mm one while using a striker mini alligator to lift the appendix. A claw forceps is inserted through the 10mm umbilical port to draw the appendix into the cannula, ensuring no transverse arrest occurs. The appendix is carefully drawn into the cannula, which is then removed from the surgical site.
